objective: Audit stuck-agent detection, recovery behavior, restart semantics, and resilience after partial child failure.
summary_findings: Stuck detection is heuristic and alert-first: claim-age >120s can false-positive, while actual recovery depends on later orphan logic and pane census.
Launcher recovery is shallow: no watchdog or restart loop, teardown does not requeue claimed work, and pane sleep can mask dead workers.
Smoke coverage is weak on recovery semantics: no stuck-path assertions, no restart/resume test, and no simulated mid-run commander failure.
Failure containment exists for blocked and commander-launch-failed states, but launch-summary PID failures are not written back into progress telemetry.
README overstates and underspecifies recovery behavior, especially around requeue conditions, teardown, and partial-complete timing.

agreement: Recovery depends heavily on monitor heuristics rather than robust launcher supervision.
Current implementation favors observability over automatic remediation.
Documentation and smoke coverage lag behind implemented failure semantics.
conflicts: No material factual conflicts across worker findings; only confidence varies on documentation severity.

blockers: Task tool did not expose background agent IDs for worker launches, so launch_started records used agent_id=unavailable and launch proof is incomplete.
Worker wkr-042-004 compared README claims against runtime files, so its audit slightly exceeded the single-file scope even though the conclusion is useful.
